1: The 'woe against the rich' (James 5:1–6) stands in the tradition of the OT prophets' economic indictments: Amos 6:1-7 (woe to those at ease in Zion; the idle rich on ivory beds); Isaiah 5:8-12 (woe to those who add house to house and field to field); Micah 2:1-2 (woe to those who covet fields and seize them). The Lukan Beatitudes add the exact counter-voice: 'Woe to you who are rich' (Luke 6:24) alongside 'Blessed are you who are poor' (Luke 6:20). James addresses the rich as outsiders — the wealthy landowners oppressing his audience, not members of his community — in a prophetic direct address that does not invite repentance but announces judgment.
2: 'Your riches have rotted, and your garments are moth-eaten.' The deterioration of stored grain (sesepe, is rotted) and clothing (setobrōta, moth-eaten) are common ancient Mediterranean images for hoarded wealth that benefits no one. Ancient wealth was stored primarily as grain, fine textiles, gold, and silver — all subject to organic decay, rust, and moth damage. The Sermon on the Mount uses the same images (Matthew 6:19-20: 'Do not store up for yourselves treasures on earth, where moths and vermin destroy'). James's point is that hoarded wealth has already effectively perished — the rich are guarding ruins.
3: 'Your gold and silver have corroded, and their corrosion will testify against you and devour your flesh like fire. You have laid up treasure in the last days.' In antiquity, gold and silver were not subject to normal corrosion — their non-corrosive nature was precisely what made them valuable as stores of wealth. James uses katiōtai (has corroded/rusted) hyperbolically or eschatologically: the impending judgment is so certain that the rust is spoken of as present fact. The corrosion becomes a legal witness (martyrion, testimony) — the very metal cries out against its hoarders. 'The last days' (en eschatais hēmerais) situates the judgment in the final period of history, which James (like other NT writers) believed had already begun.
4: 'Behold, the wages you withheld from the workers who harvested your fields are crying out against you, and the cries of the harvesters have reached the ears of the Lord of Hosts.' Withholding day-laborers' wages was a serious social crime under both Torah and Roman law: Leviticus 19:13 ('The wages of a hired worker shall not remain with you all night until the morning'); Deuteronomy 24:15 ('You shall give him his wages on the same day... for he is poor and counting on it'). Day-laborers in first-century Palestine were hired each morning in the village square (cf. Matthew 20:1-7) for agricultural work; their daily wages were their family's food for that day. Withholding them was effectively stealing food from the poor. The 'Lord of Hosts' (Kyrios Sabaoth) is the divine warrior-king who commands the armies of heaven — the title appears in the context of divine judgment against oppressors throughout the prophets (Isaiah 5:9; Isaiah 6:3).
5: 'You have lived on the earth in luxury and self-indulgence. You have fattened your hearts in a day of slaughter.' The language of 'fattening' (ethrepsate, you have nourished/fattened) applied to the rich echoes the LXX prophetic tradition: the rich who fatten themselves are like animals fattened for slaughter. Jeremiah 12:3 (LXX): 'Separate them [the wicked] like sheep for the slaughter, and sanctify them for a day of killing.' The metaphor of animals fattened for killing inverts the self-satisfaction of luxury: those who think they are enjoying abundance are actually preparing themselves for the ultimate accountability. 'A day of slaughter' is the eschatological day of judgment — the rich interpret their prosperity as blessing but are unknowingly fattening themselves for divine reckoning.
6: 'You have condemned and murdered the righteous person, who does not resist you.' The 'righteous person' (ton dikaion) who does not resist may be an individual (James himself was known as 'the Just' in early Christian tradition; Josephus records his judicial murder by the Sanhedrin), or a general pattern — the exploitative legal system that the wealthy use to crush the poor through the courts. James 2:6 establishes the pattern: the rich 'drag you into court.' The first-century Palestinian judicial system was heavily weighted toward the propertied class; day-laborers and the poor had little legal recourse against wealthy landowners who controlled the local magistrates. The non-resistance of the righteous (ouk antitassetai hymin) echoes the Servant of Isaiah 53:7 ('he was oppressed, and he was afflicted, yet he opened not his mouth').
7: 'Be patient, therefore, brothers, until the coming of the Lord. See how the farmer waits for the precious fruit of the earth, being patient about it, until it receives the early and the late rains.' The agricultural cycle of Palestine had two major rainy seasons: the 'early' (former) rains (prōimon) of October-November that softened the soil for sowing, and the 'late' rains (opsimon) of March-April that swelled the grain before harvest. Both were necessary for a successful crop; the farmer who had sown in October had to wait through the entire winter with no guarantee of the late rains. James uses this concrete agricultural rhythm — familiar to every Palestinian — as the image for eschatological patience: just as the farmer cannot rush the rains, the community cannot rush the parousia. The parousia (coming, arrival) here is James's only use of this technical eschatological term, which in the wider NT refers specifically to the return of Christ in glory.
8: 'You also be patient. Strengthen your hearts, for the coming of the Lord is near.' 'Strengthen your hearts' (stērixate tas kardias hymōn) — the heart in Jewish anthropology is the seat of will and resolve, not merely emotion. To strengthen the heart is to fortify the will for endurance through suffering. The same root (stērizō) appears in Luke 22:32 ('when you have turned back, strengthen your brothers') and Romans 16:25 ('him who is able to strengthen you'). 'Near' (ēngiken, has drawn near, perfect tense of engizō) is the same word used for the Kingdom of God's arrival in the Gospels (Mark 1:15: 'the kingdom of God has drawn near'). The perfect tense signals an arrival already begun — the Lord's coming is not merely future but has already broken into the present order.
9: 'Do not grumble against one another, brothers, so that you will not be judged. The Judge is standing right at the door.' Grumbling (stenazete, sighing/groaning against) between community members was the internal corrosion that economic pressure produces: when external injustice cannot be addressed, frustration redirects inward and poisons community relations. James's instruction to not grumble 'against one another' (kat allēlōn) directly echoes the wilderness-Israel pattern: the people who could not attack the Egyptians groaned against Moses and Aaron (Exodus 16:7-8; Numbers 14:2-4). The imminence of the Judge transforms the community's ethical horizon: the one who stands at the door (evoking the messianic expectation of Revelation 3:20 — 'I stand at the door and knock') is also the judge of community life.
10: 'As an example of suffering and patience, brothers, take the prophets who spoke in the name of the Lord.' The prophets as models of patient suffering is a standard theme in Jewish tradition. The Deuteronomistic History portrayed the prophets as the persecuted spokesmen of YHWH killed by Israel's unfaithful generations (1 Kings 18-19; 2 Chronicles 36:15-16; Nehemiah 9:26). Jesus explicitly invokes this tradition (Matthew 5:12: 'for so they persecuted the prophets before you'; Luke 13:34: 'Jerusalem, Jerusalem, who kills the prophets'). The phrase 'who spoke in the name of the Lord' (lalesantes en tō onomati Kyriou) is significant: the prophets suffered precisely because they carried the divine name, not for their own sake — their suffering was the cost of prophetic fidelity.
11: 'Behold, we count as blessed those who remained steadfast. You have heard of the steadfastness of Job, and you have seen the purpose of the Lord, how the Lord is compassionate and merciful.' Job's 'steadfastness' (hypomonē Iōb) became a byword in the Jewish tradition — though the narrative of Job shows him as anything but silent and resigned (he protests vigorously throughout). The term hypomonē (patient endurance, steadfast resistance under pressure) is not passive resignation but active endurance that refuses to abandon the relationship with God. The Testament of Job (a 1st century BCE-CE Jewish text) significantly expands Job's legendary patience. 'The purpose of the Lord' (to telos Kyriou) — the end/outcome of God's action in Job's story: restoration and vindication. 'Compassionate and merciful' (polysplagchnos kai oiktirmōn) are classical OT divine attributes (Exodus 34:6; Psalms 103:8) — James grounds the call to patience in the character of the God who has a redemptive end-goal in suffering.
12: 'But above all, my brothers, do not swear — not by heaven or by earth or by any other oath. Let your yes mean yes and your no mean no, so that you may not fall under judgment.' The instruction against oath-taking echoes Jesus's own teaching verbatim (Matthew 5:33-37: 'I say to you, do not swear at all... let what you say be simply Yes or No'). This is the closest explicit parallel in James to dominical teaching. The Jewish practice of oath-taking by heaven, earth, Jerusalem, or one's head (Matthew 5:34-36) was a way of calibrating the binding force of promises — oaths by lesser objects were considered less binding than oaths by God's name. Jesus and James reject the entire calibration system: the disciple's word must be uniformly reliable without the reinforcement of oaths.
13: 'Is anyone among you suffering? Let him pray. Is anyone cheerful? Let him sing praises.' The community's prayer-life structured by circumstance — suffering and joy both channeled into appropriate forms of address to God — reflects the Psalter's own structure: the lament psalms address God from suffering; the praise psalms address God from joy. The entire Psalter was Israel's prayer-manual for the full range of human experience. James's brief instruction maps the community's varied circumstances onto this same Psalmic framework: proseukhesthō (let him pray) for suffering; psalletō (let him sing psalms/praise) for cheerfulness. The verb psallō is specifically 'to sing a psalm' — a culturally Jewish form of praise that the early church inherited from the synagogue.
14: 'Is anyone among you sick? Let him call for the elders of the church, and let them pray over him, anointing him with oil in the name of the Lord.' The anointing of the sick with oil was practiced both as a medical treatment (the Greco-Roman world used olive oil extensively as a medicinal salve — cf. the Good Samaritan's use of oil and wine in Luke 10:34) and as a sign of divine favor and blessing (the anointing of kings and priests in Israel consecrated them to God). James does not clearly distinguish the medical from the ritual dimensions — the anointing is done 'in the name of the Lord,' which makes it a prayer-act rather than a medical procedure. The 'elders of the church' (presbyteroi tēs ekklēsias) are the recognized community leaders, whose prayer carries the weight of the community's intercession.
15: 'And the prayer offered in faith will restore the one who is sick. The Lord will raise him up, and if he has sinned, he will be forgiven.' The connection between sickness and sin reflects the Jewish assumption (contested in Job and John 9:2-3, but widespread) that physical illness could be the consequence of moral failure. James does not assert a direct causal link but acknowledges the possibility ('if he has sinned') and includes forgiveness as part of the healing process. The verb 'raise him up' (egersei, will raise) — the same word used for resurrection in the NT — may carry a double resonance: physical restoration and the eschatological raising of the dead. The passive 'will be forgiven' (aphethēsetai) is a divine passive — God is the forgiver.
16: 'Therefore confess your sins to one another and pray for one another, so that you may be healed. The prayer of a righteous person has great power as it is working.' Mutual confession of sin (exomologeisthe allelois tas hamartias) was practiced in various forms in both Jewish and early Christian communities: the Qumran community required public confession of transgressions; early Christian practice included confession before baptism (Acts 19:18) and communal discipline procedures (Matthew 18:15-20). James's instruction here is not a formal sacramental procedure but a community practice of mutual accountability that accompanies prayer for healing. 'The prayer of a righteous person has great power as it is working' (polu ischyei deesis dikaiou energoumenē) — the participle energoumenē (as it is working/energized) may be passive (the prayer energized by God) or middle (the prayer actively operating); either reading underscores that effective prayer is not passive petition but active, faith-engaged intercession.
17: 'Elijah was a human being just like us, and he prayed earnestly that it would not rain, and it did not rain on the earth for three years and six months.' Elijah (Ēlias anthrōpos ēn homoiopathes hēmin) is introduced as 'a human being of like passions/nature as us' — the phrase homoiopathes (of like passions) deliberately counters any tendency to view Elijah as a superhuman figure whose prayer power was beyond ordinary believers. The drought-period of 'three years and six months' is not given in 1 Kings 17-18 directly; 1 Kings 18:1 says 'in the third year' — the three-and-a-half years is consistent with Luke 4:25 and likely comes from Jewish tradition interpreting the drought through the apocalyptic half-week (3½ years = half of the 7-year tribulation period in Daniel). The use of Elijah as a prayer-model suggests the community understood prophetic prayer as available to ordinary believers.
18: 'Then he prayed again, and the sky gave rain, and the earth produced its crop.' The dramatic reversal — drought ended by prayer, earth restored to productivity — completes the Elijah typology and demonstrates the principle of James 5:16 by narrative example. 1 Kings 18:42-45 describes Elijah's prayer posture (bowed to the ground with his face between his knees), his persistent sending of the servant to look toward the sea (seven times), and the eventual 'small cloud like a man's hand' that became a great rain. The brevity of James's telling (James 5:17b-18) compresses the entire narrative into the essential theological point: persistent prayer by a human being of ordinary nature moved God to end a three-year drought. The eschatological framing: just as Elijah's prayer ended drought-judgment and restored the earth's fruitfulness, the community's prayer operates within the eschatological drama of the 'last days' (James 5:3).
19: 'My brothers, if anyone among you strays from the truth and someone brings him back.' The instruction about restoring the straying follows naturally from the Elijah prayer example: the prophet who prayed effectively for physical restoration of rain is the model for those who intercede practically for the spiritually straying. 'Strays from the truth' (planēthē apo tēs alētheias) — the wandering-sheep metaphor (cf. 1 Peter 2:25; Matthew 18:12-14) applied to doctrinal or moral departure from the community's foundational convictions. The context of James 5 suggests the 'straying' may include both the moral failures addressed in the prayer-and-healing section (James 5:13-16) and the economic pressures that can lead to compromise. 'Brings him back' (epistrepse auton) is the conversion/turning word — the same word used for initial repentance (Acts 3:19; James 9:35).
20: 'Let that person know that whoever turns a sinner back from his wandering path will save a soul from death and cover a multitude of sins.' The closing verse frames the entire letter's ethical concern in terms of the community's responsibility for one another's spiritual lives. 'Save a soul from death' (sōsei psychen autou ek thanatou) — whether the 'his' refers to the restorer or the restored is grammatically ambiguous, but in context it is the wanderer's soul that is rescued. 'Cover a multitude of sins' (kalypsei plethos hamartiōn) echoes Proverbs 10:12 LXX ('love covers all offenses') — the act of brotherly restoration enacts the divine forgiveness by bringing the sinner back into the community's life of grace. James ends where the gospel begins: with the rescue of the perishing and the announcement of divine forgiveness for those who return.
